title: Terraform Workspaces
learningObjectives: Create and switch between Terraform workspaces
Use terraform.workspace variable for environment-specific config
Understand workspace vs. separate configurations for environments

theory.content:
What are Workspaces?

Workspaces allow you to manage multiple, distinct state files from the same configuration. Each workspace has its own state file, making it possible to deploy the same infrastructure to different environments (dev, staging, prod) from one codebase.

Workspace Commands

# List workspaces
terraform workspace list

# Create and switch to a new workspace
terraform workspace new staging

# Switch workspace
terraform workspace select production

# Show current workspace
terraform workspace show

# Delete a workspace
terraform workspace delete staging

Using terraform.workspace

locals {
  instance_type = {
    default    = "t3.micro"
    staging    = "t3.small"
    production = "t3.large"
  }
}

resource "aws_instance" "app" {
  instance_type = local.instance_type[terraform.workspace]
  ami           = data.aws_ami.app.id
  tags = {
    Environment = terraform.workspace
  }
}

Workspace-Specific Variable Files

# Apply using workspace-specific vars
terraform apply -var-file="environments/${terraform.workspace}.tfvars"

# environments/staging.tfvars
instance_count = 2
instance_type  = "t3.small"

# environments/production.tfvars
instance_count = 10
instance_type  = "t3.large"

Workspaces vs. Separate Configurations

Workspaces work well for similar environments. For very different infrastructure between environments (different account, VPCs, architectures), consider separate root configurations with shared modules. Workspaces with a shared backend store state as:

# S3 backend with workspaces
# Default: env:/default/app/terraform.tfstate  
# Staging: env:/staging/app/terraform.tfstate
# Prod:    env:/production/app/terraform.tfstate

Workspaces Documentation →
